summary: Re-verify the rate-limited tarot/divination reference claims with fresh adversarial web-search voters

reverified.claim: I Ching yarrow-stalk divination uses 50 stalks, with one set aside and not used, leaving 49 to be manipulated through repeated division to build a hexagram.
notes: SUPPORT (high): Multiple authoritative sources including Wikibooks, Wikipedia, and specialized I Ching divination sites all confirm that the traditional yarrow-stalk method uses exactly 50 stalks, with one set aside unused, leaving 49 stalks that are repeatedly divided and counted to build hexagrams.

SUPPORT (high): Wikipedia's I Ching divination article explicitly states: "Fifty yarrow stalks are used, though one stalk is set aside at the beginning and takes no further part in the process of consultation. The remaining forty-nine stalks are roughly sorted into two piles..." This practice is based on the Great Commentary (c. 300 BC) and the reconstruction by Neo-Confucian scholar Zhu Xi, which remains the standard method used today.

reverified.claim: Ifá/Yoruba divination is performed by trained, initiated priests called babaláwo (male) and ìyánífá (female) — a credentialed, role-restricted practice.
notes: SUPPORT (high): Wikipedia (Babalawo), Ileifa.org, and academic sources document that Ifá divination is performed exclusively by formally initiated priests (babaláwo/ìyánífá) who undergo 5+ years of apprenticeship, memorize sacred verses, and complete mandatory initiation ceremonies (Ìtẹ̀fá); historical gender-based role restrictions are documented across sources, though some variation exists by region and lineage.

SUPPORT (high): Wikipedia, academic sources, and Yoruba tradition specialists confirm that babaláwo and ìyánífá are formally trained, initiated, and credentialed practitioners with role restrictions—with 3-15 year initiation processes involving memorization of 256 Odù verses, ritual ceremonies, and formal credentialing requirements. The terminology (male/female roles) is also verified.

reverified.claim: Ifá uses a fixed corpus of 256 binary signs (Odu), organized as sixteen major categories/books, cast via a divining chain (Ọ̀pẹ̀lẹ̀) or sacred palm nuts (Ikin).
notes: SUPPORT (high): UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage listing and peer-reviewed academic research (ScienceDirect, 2023) confirm all elements: 256 binary Odu organized in 16 principal categories, cast via Ọ̀pẹ̀lẹ̀ divining chain or Ikin sacred palm nuts.
SUPPORT (high): UNESCO and academic sources confirm all major claims: 256 Odu as binary signs, 16 principal categories, divining via Ọ̀pẹ̀lẹ̀ chain and Ikin palm nuts, cast on Ọpọ́n Ifá tray.

reverified.claim: In Forer's 1948 experiment, 39 students each received an identical generic personality sketch (assembled partly from an astrology book) and rated its accuracy at about 4.3 out of 5.
notes: SUPPORT (high): Forer (1949) published the study showing 39 students rated identical astrology-based personality sketches at an average of 4.26-4.30 out of 5 on accuracy, matching the claim's "about 4.3 out of 5." Details confirmed across multiple academic sources including ResearchGate, Wikipedia, and the APA PsycNET database.

SUPPORT (high): Multiple authoritative sources (Wikipedia, psychology reference sites, academic sources) confirm all key elements: 39 students, identical sketch from astrology source, average rating of 4.26-4.30 out of 5.

reverified.claim: The Barnum/Forer effect depends on statement vagueness: generic, broadly-applicable statements let people project their own specifics onto the text, raising perceived accuracy.
notes: SUPPORT (high): EBSCO Research Starters (authoritative reference work) and multiple peer-reviewed sources confirm vagueness is central to the Forer effect: "One of the most important factors when reproducing this study is to keep the statements as vague as possible." Forer's 1948 original study explicitly demonstrated that identical generic statements received 4.26/5 accuracy ratings, and research confirms projection of personal specifics onto vague statements drives perceived accuracy.

SUPPORT (high): Bertram Forer's 1948-1949 experiments and comprehensive reviews (Dickson & Kelly 1985) consistently demonstrate that the Forer/Barnum effect fundamentally depends on vagueness, with people projecting their own interpretations onto generic statements to create perceived accuracy (average rating 4.26/5 for identical horoscope-derived profiles).

reverified.claim: The Urim and Thummim in ancient Israel functioned largely as a binary (yes/no) oracle for consulting divine will, and access was socially restricted to high-status figures (king, priest, generals) rather than ordinary people.
notes: SUPPORT (high): Multiple authoritative sources (Jewish Encyclopedia, Wikipedia, Bible Study Tools, Chabad.org) confirm the Urim and Thummim functioned as a binary yes/no oracle with access restricted to kings, high priests, judges, and military leaders for matters of public welfare—not for ordinary people.

SUPPORT (high): Multiple authoritative sources including JewishEncyclopedia.com, Encyclopedia.com, and academic biblical scholarship sources confirm both that the Urim and Thummim functioned as a binary yes/no oracle and that access was restricted to high-status figures (king, high court, generals, priests) for matters of national importance rather than ordinary people.

reverified.claim: Whitson & Galinsky (2008, Science, "Lacking Control Increases Illusory Pattern Perception") showed that experimentally reducing a sense of control increases the tendency to perceive illusory patterns in noise, form illusory correlations, and adopt superstitions.
notes: SUPPORT (high): Whitson & Galinsky (2008, Science 322:115-117) published peer-reviewed research with six experiments explicitly demonstrating that experimentally reduced control increases illusory pattern perception, illusory correlations, and superstitious beliefs, confirmed by PubMed, National Geographic, and Columbia Business School sources.

SUPPORT (high): Whitson & Galinsky (2008) in Science, volume 322, pages 115-117 published experiments demonstrating that lack of control increases illusory pattern perception, illusory correlations, and superstitious behavior. Study verified across multiple authoritative sources including the journal Science, PubMed, and institutional repositories.

reverified.claim: Brian Eno and Peter Schmidt's "Oblique Strategies" is a deck of cards used to introduce randomness/constraint to break habitual thinking in creative work — a documented aleatory creativity method.
notes: SUPPORT (high): Wikipedia, Open Culture, and multiple educational sources confirm that Oblique Strategies is a 1975 card deck by Eno and Schmidt designed to introduce randomness and constraints to break creative blocks—a documented aleatory method with roots in Eastern philosophy and John Cage's chance techniques.

SUPPORT (high): Wikipedia confirms Oblique Strategies is a card deck (7x9cm cards in a black box) created by Brian Eno and Peter Schmidt in 1975. Wikipedia's Peter Schmidt article states the cards were designed for users to "employ them as a complete set or draw single cards when facing creative dilemmas," introducing randomness through shuffled drawing. The original description notes cards "can be trusted even if their appropriateness is quite unclear," confirming they use randomness/constraint to break habitual thinking. Wikipedia's Aleatoric Art article defines aleatory methods as those using chance ("determined by throwing dice"), and Oblique Strategies' shuffled-draw mechanism fits this methodology, though Wikipedia doesn't explicitly label it "aleatory creativity" — the functional mechanisms documented clearly support this characterization.
